APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new structured prediction model (multigraphs) for overlapping NER, provides algorithmic analysis (O(nT) inference), and evaluates on standard benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) against strong baselines. From an experimental-methodology perspective the work uses expected metrics (P/R/F1), reports hyperparameters, and demonstrates empirical gains, making it directly relevant to the venue’s audience of researchers working on structured prediction and information extraction. The only minor concerns are that code release is not mentioned and the model does not model type interactions, but these do not detract from its overall fit for a top conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (overlapping NER) with a novel structured-prediction formulation (multigraphs) and claims both theoretical parity in complexity with prior mention-hypergraph work and empirical gains on standard benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA). The methodology (log-linear model, inside–outside inference, MPE decoding), comparison to strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parsing), and standard metrics align with expectations for a top NLP/ML venue. Its audience—researchers in structured prediction and entity recognition—will find the conceptual advance and empirical results directly relevant, and the positioning and datasets used make it well situated for broad impact in the community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser), and reports hyperparameters and a claimed O(nT) inference algorithm — suggesting the main technical and empirical arguments are plausible. However, important reproducibility and robustness details are missing in the summary: code release is not mentioned, there is no note of statistical significance testing or empirical runtime/memory comparisons, and the model’s inability to capture cross-type interactions is a reported limitation. These are minor but relevant issues that do not obviously overturn the results, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): Given the summary, the paper presents internally consistent technical claims: a log-linear multigraph model trained by likelihood with a generalized inside–outside and max-product decoding that achieves O(nT) inference akin to mention hypergraphs, and reports empirical gains on ACE/GNIA against appropriate baselines. The mathematical apparatus (inside–outside DP, MPE via max-product) is plausible and described at the required level for correctness; experimental methodology appears sound with strong baselines and hyperparameter reporting. Minor technical concerns remain: no mention of statistical significance testing and the model explicitly omits type–type interactions (a limitation relative to linear-chain CRFs) which could affect some comparisons. These issues do not invalidate the main technical contribution, so I rate it slightly below perfect.

However, important reproducibility and robustness details are missing in the summary: code release is not mentioned, there is no note of statistical significance testing or empirical runtime/memory comparisons, and the model’s inability to capture cross-type interactions is a reported limitation. These are minor but relevant issues that do not obviously overturn the results, so I rate it a 4.

These issues do not invalidate the main technical contribution, so I rate it slightly below perfect.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—overlapping entity recognition—and proposes a principled multigraph structured-prediction model that reportedly improves F1 (up to 2.1 points on ACE2004) while preserving linear-time O(nT) inference, which should materially influence method choice in this area. It is evaluated against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parsers) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA), suggesting empirical relevance to both researchers and practitioners. However, the contribution stops short of being transformative because it omits modeling interactions between entity types, and reproducibility/practical adoption may be hindered (code availability not mentioned), limiting immediate widespread uptake. | B(4): This paper addresses an important, practical problem—overlapping entity recognition—and proposes a novel multigraph structured-prediction model that preserves linear-time O(nT) inference while improving empirical F1 (up to 2.1 points on ACE2004) over strong baselines like mention hypergraphs and constituency parsers. From my perspective this will change how researchers think about expressiveness vs. efficiency trade-offs in overlapping NER: the multigraph formulation offers a clear alternative that is both computationally attractive and empirically effective. It also opens concrete research directions (extensions to model cross-type interactions, integration with downstream pipelines, and broader multigraph representations) and is likely to be built upon by the community, though its impact is somewhat tempered by the stated limitation that it currently does not model interactions between distinct entity types.

It is evaluated against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parsers) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA), suggesting empirical relevance to both researchers and practitioners. However, the contribution stops short of being transformative because it omits modeling interactions between entity types, and reproducibility/practical adoption may be hindered (code availability not mentioned), limiting immediate widespread uptake.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and uses common metrics (P/R/F1) with sensible baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency-parser), and reports hyperparameters—supporting largely fair comparisons. However, it appears to omit a directly relevant contemporary multigraph-based method (e.g., the 2017 "Mention Separators" work) that addresses overlapping mentions with similar representations and exact inference; claiming to be the first structured multigraph approach without engaging that prior work weakens the comparative contribution. Differences vs. baselines are empirically shown (F1 gains reported), but the absence of that key competing approach and no code release make reproduction and full assessment of relative gains difficult. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency-parser approaches) on standard overlapping-entity datasets using the same metrics, and reports complexity and empirical differences—so comparisons are largely fair. However, the authors claim novelty as the first multigraph-based structured model despite an important prior multigraph-style approach (e.g., 'Mention Separators', 2017) that directly targets overlapping mentions; omission of direct comparison or discussion of that work weakens the literature comparison. The paper does analyze algorithmic complexity versus baselines and reports when it outperforms them, but it does not fully acknowledge or benchmark against all most relevant competing multigraph methods, which is a significant but not fatal omission.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from problem motivation to the multigraph method, inference, and experiments (ACE2004/2005, GENIA). The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good with consistent notation, effective figures/tables/examples, and thorough related-work coverage. A few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., more explicit pointers to implementation details and code release would aid reproducibility), but these do not impede understanding. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (a multigraph-based structured model for overlapping entities with O(nT) inference). Notation and algorithms are presented clearly enough to describe training (regularized log-likelihood, L-BFGS) and inference/decoding (generalized inside–outside and max-product), and figures/tables (experiments on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA vs. strong baselines) effectively support the claims. Related work is covered thoroughly and writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., the detailed dynamic-programming derivation) could be streamlined for readers less familiar with mention hypergraphs or constituency approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from problem motivation to the multigraph method, inference, and experiments (ACE2004/2005, GENIA). The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good with consistent notation, effective figures/tables/examples, and thorough related-work coverage. A few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., more explicit pointers to implementation details and code release would aid reproducibility), but these do not impede understanding.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (a multigraph-based structured model for overlapping entities with O(nT) inference). Notation and algorithms are presented clearly enough to describe training (regularized log-likelihood, L-BFGS) and inference/decoding (generalized inside–outside and max-product), and figures/tables (experiments on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA vs. strong baselines) effectively support the claims. Related work is covered thoroughly and writing quality is good, though a few sections (e.g., the detailed dynamic-programming derivation) could be streamlined for readers less familiar with mention hypergraphs or constituency approaches.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Given the provided summary and the closely related 2017 work that already uses a multigraph representation for overlapping mentions, the paper’s core idea—using multigraphs for overlapping entity recognition—is not genuinely new. The authors appear to contribute technical refinements (a log-linear training objective, L-BFGS optimization, and an inside–outside style DP for inference), which are useful but represent incremental extensions rather than a fundamentally novel perspective. The claim that this is the first structured-prediction multigraph model is contradicted by prior work (e.g., "Mention Separators" 2017), so the overall novelty is limited. | B(3): The paper presents a clear and useful application of a multigraph structured-prediction formulation to overlapping-entity recognition and reports empirical gains, but the core modeling ideas (multigraph representations with generalized inside–outside inference and log-linear training) appear to closely mirror earlier work (e.g., 2017 "mention separators"/multigraph approaches). While the authors articulate algorithmic details and achieve stronger empirical results than mention hypergraphs, the technical contributions read as non-trivial engineering and adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. From the perspective of an expert in overlapping-entity formalisms, this is a moderate novelty—interesting and well-differentiated experimentally, but not a breakthrough over closely related prior multigraph/mention-separator methods.

While the authors articulate algorithmic details and achieve stronger empirical results than mention hypergraphs, the technical contributions read as non-trivial engineering and adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates on three standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and compares to appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser), so empirical claims about improved F1 have reasonable dataset and baseline coverage. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported to isolate which modeling choices drive gains (only hyperparameters are listed), and runtime/memory measurements or code release are not mentioned despite an O(nT) complexity claim—this hinders reproducibility and practical assessment. Related work appears thorough, but the summary omits explicit comparison/discussion of a very close prior multigraph-style approach (e.g., 2017 mention-separators paper), and though a limitation (no inter-type interactions) is stated, failure cases and negative results are not deeply analyzed. Overall the experiments are adequate to show promise but miss key analyses (ablations, efficiency, released code) needed for a fully convincing and reproducible evaluation. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is reasonably comprehensive: the model is compared to strong baselines across three standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004, ACE2005, GENIA) and shows clear F1 improvements, supporting the main empirical claims. However, the paper appears to lack thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses exploring design choices (only hyperparameters are reported), which weakens understanding of which components drive gains. Related work is described as thorough, but the authors' claim to be the first multigraph-structured predictor is contradicted by closely related prior work (e.g., 2017 mention-separator multigraph approaches), so the coverage/claim needs clarification. The paper does acknowledge a notable limitation (no modeling of interactions between entity types), and training/inference details (objective, optimizer, complexity) are provided, but absence of released code and limited analysis of failure cases reduce reproducibility and diagnostic depth.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on three standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and compares to appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser), so empirical claims about improved F1 have reasonable dataset and baseline coverage. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported to isolate which modeling choices drive gains (only hyperparameters are listed), and runtime/memory measurements or code release are not mentioned despite an O(nT) complexity claim—this hinders reproducibility and practical assessment. Related work appears thorough, but the summary omits explicit comparison/discussion of a very close prior multigraph-style approach (e.g., 2017 mention-separators paper), and though a limitation (no inter-type interactions) is stated, failure cases and negative results are not deeply analyzed. Overall the experiments are adequate to show promise but miss key analyses (ablations, efficiency, released code) needed for a fully convincing and reproducible evaluation.

However, the paper appears to lack thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses exploring design choices (only hyperparameters are reported), which weakens understanding of which components drive gains. Related work is described as thorough, but the authors' claim to be the first multigraph-structured predictor is contradicted by closely related prior work (e.g., 2017 mention-separator multigraph approaches), so the coverage/claim needs clarification. The paper does acknowledge a notable limitation (no modeling of interactions between entity types), and training/inference details (objective, optimizer, complexity) are provided, but absence of released code and limited analysis of failure cases reduce reproducibility and diagnostic depth.

main_claims: Proposes a new multigraph-based structured prediction model capable of recognizing overlapping entities.
This is the first structured prediction model (according to the authors) utilizing multigraphs to predict overlapping structures.
The multigraph model achieves the same linear time complexity O(nT) as the mention hypergraph model while yielding significantly better empirical results (up to 2.1 F1 improvement on ACE2004).
The model outperforms prior baselines including the mention hypergraph model, linear-chain CRF, and constituency-parser-based approaches on standard overlapping-entity datasets.
_summary.methodology: A log-linear multigraph-based model where entities are represented via multigraph structures; trained by maximizing a regularized log-likelihood (L-BFGS optimization), inference uses a generalized inside-outside dynamic programming procedure with O(nT) time complexity, and decoding performs MPE via a max-product procedure to extract highest-scoring subgraphs.
limitations: The model does not model interactions between distinct entity types (unlike the linear-chain CRF baseline).

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years designing and evaluating structured prediction models in NLP and information extraction; experience implementing production-ready sequence and graph-based systems and running large-scale empirical studies.
traits: examines experimental setup for completeness: datasets, splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, and training details
requires strong baselines, clear ablation studies, and significance testing to support empirical claims
values reproducibility: looks for code release, runtime/complexity measurements, and implementation details that enable replication
checks optimization and inference claims carefully (e.g., complexity proofs vs. practical runtime and memory usage)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and implementation rigorously support the paper's empirical claims and whether the work can be reproduced and deployed.

reviewer_b.name: Domain & Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor studying information extraction and structured prediction who has authored work on overlapping entity representations and closely follows literature on NER architectures and linguistic applications.
traits: assesses novelty and relationship to prior work, including conceptual differences from mention hypergraphs and constituency-based methods
evaluates broader significance: whether the modeling advance addresses important limitations and how it might influence future research directions
looks for clarity in theoretical contributions and whether complexity/expressiveness trade-offs are well-motivated
notices practical applicability to downstream tasks and datasets beyond standard benchmarks
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper primarily on novelty, clarity of conceptual contribution, and potential impact on the overlapping-entity recognition research trajectory.
